sound_type: midnight chattering -- nocturnal colony vocalization; hundreds to thousands of birds calling from burrows, rock crevices, and in flight at night; churring/purring/chattering multi-phrase calls; acoustic mass through numbers; E=6
ecosystem: Offshore island -- remote oceanic islands, rocky outcrops, headlands off Pacific Northwest coast; pelagic forager; nests in burrows and rock crevices on predator-free islands
season: Summer (breeding; arrives April-May; chicks fledge August-September; rest of year at sea in open Pacific)
conservation_status: Vulnerable (IUCN). Documented colony declines. Primary threat: introduced predators (rats, American Mink) on breeding islands. Secondary: light attraction mortality (offshore platforms, lighthouses). Tertiary: climate change affecting offshore prey. Island biosecurity (predator eradication) is primary management tool.

coordinates.description: Triangle Island, British Columbia (50,000-100,000 breeding pairs; largest eastern Pacific colony). Three Arch Rocks NWR, Oregon. Destruction Island, Washington. Pelagic range: entire North Pacific basin, southward to subtropical waters in winter.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Complex multi-phrase call: churring, purring, chattering sequences. Rapid staccato; male and female calls differ. Functions: mate attraction, individual recognition, burrow location.
acoustic_data.colony_environment: Hundreds to thousands of birds calling simultaneously from burrows (muffled, subterranean), rock crevices, and in flight overhead. All in darkness. Calls appear to come from everywhere. Daytime colony is silent.
acoustic_data.temporal_restriction: Night only. Gulls cannot hunt in darkness; storm-petrels exploit temporal niche to avoid predation. Daylight colony = complete silence and apparent emptiness.
acoustic_data.e6_calibration: E=6: acoustic mass via numbers (hundreds to thousands of 35-55 g birds), not individual amplitude. Collective colony effect is substantial but each individual is small. Compare: Elephant Seal bull (E=8, single massive animal) vs. Storm-Petrel colony (E=6, thousands of tiny birds).

morphology.size: 7-8 inches (18-21 cm); wingspan 17-19 inches (44-48 cm); weight 35-55 g
morphology.appearance: Dark brown-black; white rump patch (diagnostic); forked tail (vs. Wilson's square tail); pale wing bars
morphology.flight_style: Erratic, bounding, moth-like; irregular wingbeats with glides; direction changes unpredictably

nodes.title: First Hydrobatidae -- Storm-Petrels Enter the Engine
nodes.description: Degree 286 is the first Hydrobatidae (storm-petrel family) in the engine. Smallest seabirds that venture into the open Pacific. Pelagic for most of the year; nocturnal at breeding colonies. The entry extends the engine into the offshore island and pelagic ocean acoustic environments.

nodes.title: Nocturnal Colony Strategy -- Temporal Predator Avoidance
nodes.description: Storm-petrels arrive at and depart from breeding burrows only at night, avoiding gull predation (gulls cannot hunt in darkness). Daytime colony = silence. Night colony = cacophony from hundreds/thousands of birds. One of the Pacific Northwest's most extraordinary and least-encountered wildlife soundscapes.

nodes.title: Petrel = Peter: Walking on Water
nodes.description: 'Petrel' derives from French 'Pétrel' (diminutive of Pierre/Peter), referring to the bird's surface-pattering foraging behavior -- appearing to walk on water like St. Peter on the Sea of Galilee. 'Storm-petrel': association with approaching bad weather. 'Mother Carey's Chickens': sailor mythology for souls of drowned sailors.

nodes.title: Triangle Island BC -- 50,000-100,000 Breeding Pairs
nodes.description: Triangle Island, approximately 25 km off northern Vancouver Island tip. Largest Leach's Storm-Petrel colony in the eastern Pacific. The midnight colony here is one of the most remarkable wildlife soundscapes in the Pacific Northwest. Inaccessible except by research vessel.

cross_references.relationship: E=8, Phocidae. Block contrast: 1,500-2,500 lb animal (E=8) vs. 35-55 g bird colony (E=6). Individual massive power vs. collective tiny mass.
E=7. First Phalacrocoracidae. Both colonial. Storm-petrel colony nocturnal vs. Brandt's daytime. Hydrobatidae vs. Phalacrocoracidae. Both offshore island breeders.
E=9. First S36 maximum. E=9 + E=6 = E=15. The most confrontational S36 entry paired with one of the most hidden SPS entries. Both require full commitment to the specific mode to experience.
